rationales.self_direction: He is seriously considering switching labs again because the new project is 'so much more interesting,' prioritizing his own curiosity over the path already set by his committee.
rationales.stimulation: He lights up at 'VR and neural mapping,' 'fieldwork in Costa Rica,' and the chance to be in a rainforest instead of routine sequencing, showing a strong pull toward novelty and challenge.
rationales.security: He is tempted to 'uproot everything again' for a project he admits he does not 'even fully know,' favoring uncertainty over a stable setup.
rationales.conformity: He pushes against the expectation to stay put, bristling at 'what will your committee think?' and questioning how anyone can just do one thing.

rationales.stimulation: His attention keeps jumping to 'flights to New Zealand,' 'pottery classes,' and learning to surf, and he says his brain 'doesn't want to stay in one place.'
rationales.achievement: When Dr. Sharma asked about his thesis timeline, he had 'literally nothing' to say and cannot stay on his analysis, which undercuts sustained progress.
rationales.security: He has no thesis direction and fears his mother will learn he 'might not actually finish it,' showing instability around a supposedly secure path.
rationales.conformity: He knows he is not meeting academic and family expectations, admitting Amma will be 'so disappointed' and that he cannot do what everyone else seems able to do.
rationales.benevolence: By hiding that he 'hasn't figured anything out' while his mother tells everyone about the PhD, he is failing to protect a close relationship from avoidable hurt.

rationales.self_direction: He does not delete the message and keeps the 'little door' open even though 'everyone' would say to stay in the PhD, showing willingness to author his own path.
rationales.stimulation: A Singapore startup, a rooftop garden, a young team, and the idea of more 'velocity' all clearly energize him far more than routine paper reading.
rationales.achievement: He spends an hour on company videos 'instead of reading papers,' pulling attention away from the work he is supposed to advance.
rationales.security: The opportunity is a 'random job' that could mean leaving his PhD midstream, so he is actively entertaining a less predictable future.

rationales.self_direction: He chooses to do the interview and keep moving through the process even though leaving the lab he just transferred into would be hard to justify.
rationales.stimulation: He is energized by a role building 'something new' and by being valued for the 'weird' project he built for fun, which fits his attraction to novelty.
rationales.conformity: He keeps the interview secret because he knows people in the program will think he is 'not serious,' showing he is moving against the expectations around him.

rationales.stimulation: The second interview has him so charged up that his 'heart is doing weird things,' showing how much this new possibility is driving him.
rationales.achievement: He says he 'definitely bombed' the committee meeting because he could not focus, which directly undercuts competent performance.

rationales.self_direction: He is seriously considering a move where he would build a data pipeline from scratch in Singapore rather than stay in the PhD, weighing a path that is clearly his own.
rationales.stimulation: The offer centers on relocation, a new country, and a role starting from scratch, all of which keep his attention far more than lab work.
rationales.security: He describes the choice as a 'LinkedIn message away from blowing up my life again' and notes having 'no savings,' showing how destabilizing it could be.
rationales.tradition: Taking the startup offer would break from the established PhD route his family has been celebrating, and he knows it will be hard to explain to them.
rationales.benevolence: He lies to Amma that research is 'going great' and calls himself a 'terrible son,' signaling that his choices are straining his obligations to close family.

rationales.self_direction: He frames the move as 'run the experiment' to find out what fits instead of letting the current track decide for him.
rationales.stimulation: What keeps pulling him is the chance to know startup life and Singapore firsthand rather than stay with 'pure hypothesis.'
rationales.security: He knows that if he leaves and it fails, 'I can't just come back,' so he is actively weighing a much less protected path.
rationales.benevolence: He turns down Aditya's dinner invitation, says he has been doing that 'a lot lately,' and calls himself a 'terrible friend' and 'terrible son.'

rationales.self_direction: He makes the decision himself, writing 'Decided to accept the offer. Sent the email,' which is a direct act of choosing his own path.
rationales.stimulation: Accepting a January move to Singapore for a startup role is a clear step toward something new and unpredictable.
rationales.security: By leaving the PhD for an overseas startup, he is giving up a more established track for a riskier one.

rationales.self_direction: Even after expecting anger, he follows through and stays with the choice that feels like he has been 'searching for something,' rather than returning to the old path.
rationales.stimulation: He describes a 'fizzy feeling' like when 'something NEW' is coming, showing how strongly novelty is driving the decision.
rationales.tradition: The conversation with his parents turns on 'so no PhD?' and he continues anyway, stepping away from the expected academic path.
rationales.benevolence: He handles the news directly with Dr. Sharma, his parents, and Aditya, and the entry stays closely attuned to those relationships rather than treating the move as purely individual.

rationales.self_direction: He is still moving forward with the relocation even after finding the old promise to 'definitely finish my PhD,' choosing the life he now wants over the one he once pledged to follow.
rationales.stimulation: The fact that he is in the middle of packing for Singapore keeps the entry oriented around a major new chapter rather than staying put.
rationales.tradition: Leaving behind the old all-caps promise to 'definitely finish my PhD' shows a break from the established path he once took as a given.

rationales.self_direction: He says he has interrogated the decision 'from every angle' and still needs to move, showing an active choice rather than passive drift.
rationales.stimulation: He explicitly values that Singapore will be 'something different,' which is the main appeal he keeps returning to.
rationales.security: He rejects staying for 'safety' and chooses a path whose outcome he cannot know in advance.

rationales.hedonism: He turns his phone off, cooks shakshuka 'from scratch,' eats slowly with coffee, and says 'I needed it,' clearly prioritizing rest and sensory enjoyment.
Even with good wine and music, he keeps checking Slack and finally opens the laptop, saying he felt 'relieved' to stop pretending he was relaxing.
rationales.achievement: He keeps doing the math on whether he 'finish[ed] enough' and works another two hours, showing strong pressure to meet performance standards.
rationales.benevolence: He explains that if he is not working, 'someone on my team might be stuck,' so he is prioritizing his team's needs over his evening.

rationales.hedonism: After a ten-hour day he wants to run a bath, but instead goes straight back to prep and says he could not even argue he 'deserved an hour off.'
rationales.achievement: He keeps working after exhaustion to prepare for 'tomorrow's review,' putting performance ahead of recovery.

rationales.hedonism: He uses the forty-five-minute gap to get a cortado, sit at the window, and enjoy that 'small and quiet but nice' pause without a laptop or phone.
He goes to a restaurant he had wanted to try, but spends 'half the meal thinking about the quarterly review deck' and leaves feeling he was not really there.

rationales.conformity: She describes conferences as a 'careful dance' of saying 'all the right things' so nobody complains, and ends satisfied that 'nobody left upset.'

rationales.self_direction: She worries that being 'the person everyone expects me to be' may mean giving up the more approachable teacher she wants to be.
rationales.conformity: She 'followed protocol exactly' and did everything 'by the book' even though it cost her the student's trust.

rationales.tradition: She treats her mother's suggestions about 'that nice man from church' as an ongoing family script, saying 'it's just what mothers do' rather than rejecting it outright.

rationales.self_direction: She admits she may skip the ceramics class because of her parents' reaction and says she has 'started to forget what I want when no one's watching.'
rationales.conformity: She is known as the one who 'doesn't argue in meetings,' plans to smile politely at Teacher Lin, and avoids the class because she does not want to upset her parents.
rationales.tradition: She frames the conflict in family terms, saying 'that's not how my family works' and 'we show up for each other,' which keeps inherited expectations in charge.

rationales.self_direction: She picks up the registration form but still may not sign up, and she cannot even raise the topic with her mother, showing how little room she gives her own wants.
rationales.security: She talks herself toward the existing arrangement by emphasizing a 'stable job,' 'parents who care,' 'a routine that works,' and the cost of the class.
rationales.conformity: Her toast goes exactly as 'it was supposed to be,' she responds politely to Teacher Lin, and she centers Sunday dinners and family expectations over the hobby.
